draft.introduction: Fragment 12 introduces Anaxagoras’s concept of Nous (Mind) as the ordering principle of the cosmos. Unlike the material elements, Nous is unmixed and self-ruling, and it initiated the cosmic rotation that separated all things.

translation.text: All other things have a share of everything, but Mind is unlimited and self-ruling and is mixed with no thing, but is alone by itself. Mind has all knowledge about everything and the greatest power; and Mind rules all things that have soul, both the greater and the lesser.
translation.original_text: τὰ μὲν ἄλλα παντὸς μοῖραν μετέχει, νοῦς δέ ἐστιν ἄπειρον καὶ αὐτοκρατὲς καὶ μέμεικται οὐδενὶ χρήματι, ἀλλὰ μόνος αὐτὸς ἐπ᾽ ἑωυτοῦ ἐστιν

translator_notes.note: Aristotle praised Anaxagoras for introducing Nous as a cosmic cause but criticized him for using it only as a last resort when mechanical explanations failed. Plato made a similar complaint in the Phaedo (97b–99d).
